principle: Peripheral vision is used more than central vision
characteristics: Peripheral vision identifies scene type and detects movement/danger faster; central vision is for detail. Users rely on it for initial assessment and visual anchors.
dos: Ensure the periphery communicates site purpose; use high-quality thumbnails; place elements for 'glanceable' info in the periphery; use peripheral cues to draw attention to changes.
donts: Don't place vital info where it can be overlooked due to banner blindness; don't use heavy peripheral animation that distracts from the primary task; don't assume users only see the center focus.

principle: The Limbic System drives decision making
characteristics: Decisions are controlled by the part of the brain that does not control language; it is based on 'feeling' and the 'Why'.
dos: Communicate the purpose/benefit ('Why') before the features ('What').
donts: Don't rely solely on facts and technical features to persuade users.

principle: The human body is built on the proportions of the Golden Section.
characteristics: Ancient and modern architectural canons are linked to human measures (ell, thumb, foot).
dos: Use scale and proportion derived from human body ratios (Modulor) to design spaces.
donts: Ignore human eye level (avg. 170 cm) when placing visual information.

principle: Medium type width is the easiest type to read.
characteristics: Optimal balance between character width and spacing (condensed vs. expanded).
dos: Choose medium-width typefaces for primary text body.
donts: Use extra-condensed or extra-expanded type for long reading passages.

characteristics: Visual hierarchy is the most effective tool for making a design look 'designed'.
dos: De-emphasize secondary and tertiary information to highlight what matters.
donts: Don't let every element compete for attention creating a 'wall of content'.

principle: People see cues that tell them what to do with an object
characteristics: Affordances are perceived action possibilities (e.g., a button's shadow suggests pushing).
dos: Use shading and shadows to show when an object is clickable or active.
donts: Use hover cues for touch-only devices like the iPad.

principle: A Button Should Look Like a Button
characteristics: Interactive elements must be visually distinct and follow familiar forms to be recognized as actionable.
dos: Use rectangles or rounded rectangles; ensure the button stands out from the background.
donts: Use organic or irregular shapes that dissolve the function; remove too many visual cues so it looks like plain text.

principle: There's a special part of the brain just for recognizing faces
characteristics: The fusiform face area (FFA) processes faces faster than objects and is linked to the emotional center.
dos: Use up-close faces looking at the camera for emotional impact; use faces looking at products to direct attention.
donts: Assume autistic users process faces using the FFA.

principle: Normal spacing between letters is essential for legibility in photo-typesetting.
characteristics: Too narrow a setting of letters makes reading difficult.
dos: Insist on normal spacing (spationierung) when ordering photo-typesetting.
donts: Use narrow or irregular letter spacing that impairs word recognition.

principle: The right width of column is essential for an even reading rhythm.
characteristics: Optimal line length prevents fatigue; usually 7 to 10 words per line for normal reading distance.
dos: Calculate column width based on typeface size and a 30-35 cm reading distance.
donts: Use overlong lines (strenuous to track) or overshort lines (energy wasted on frequent line changes).

principle: Good leading carries the eye optically from one line to the next.
characteristics: Vertical distance between lines provides stability and confidence during reading.
dos: Use sufficient leading to prevent the eye from taking in neighboring lines at the same time.
donts: Set lines too narrowly (impairs speed) or too widely (breaks link between lines).

principle: Information presented with clear and logically set out hierarchies is better understood and retained.
characteristics: Clear titles, subtitles, texts, illustrations, and captions facilitate faster reading and better memorability.
dos: Organize text and pictures systematically; use clear and logically set out titles and captions.
donts: Mix different styles of typefaces (e.g., Helvetica with Univers) or use inconsistent positioning.

principle: Contrast between type sizes must be clearly recognizable.
characteristics: Clear differences in size and weight (e.g., 9-pt vs 6-pt) facilitate categorization of information.
dos: Use distinct colour values (light grey for medium, deep grey for bold) to differentiate text levels.
donts: Mix characters of the same style/family (e.g., Helvetica with Univers) in a way that creates ambiguity.

characteristics: Use 'has a' relationships instead of 'is a' relationships to avoid combinatorial explosion of subclasses.
dos: Delegate behaviors to other objects; use composition/aggregation; replace behavior at runtime.
donts: Extend classes in several independent dimensions (e.g., cargo type x engine type); rely solely on parallel inheritance hierarchies.

characteristics: Identify the aspects of your application that vary and separate them from what stays the same to minimize effects caused by changes.
dos: Isolate parts of the program that vary in independent modules; divide logic into independent compartments.
donts: Mix tax calculation code or varying logic with the rest of a method's primary code.

principle: DesignOps mindset: Focus on the 'how' so design can focus on the 'what'.
characteristics: Operationalizing, optimizing, and scaling design through standardized processes.
dos: Use common naming structures for files; standardize toolstacks; conduct discovery research on team pain points.
donts: Let talent leave due to boredom or repetitive manual tasks; ignore the human experience of the team.

characteristics: Users are better at recognizing previously experienced items than recalling them from memory.
dos: Show recently viewed items; provide clear thumbnails and labels; use familiar icons.
donts: Force users to remember specific paths or item details across screens.

characteristics: Every screen or section must have a single most important action or message.
dos: Make the primary CTA visually distinct (e.g., 'Boss' button); hide secondary actions in menus.
donts: Compete for attention with multiple equally styled primary buttons.

characteristics: Users value efficiency and speed in achieving their goals.
dos: Make interactions short, simple, and fast; remove needless parts; use clear signposting.
donts: Add friction with unnecessary steps or complex UI decorations that don't serve the task.

characteristics: Design should allow users to explore without fear of making mistakes.
dos: Make buttons large enough to tap easily; use undo options instead of complex confirmations.
donts: Place destructive actions (Delete) next to frequent positive actions.

characteristics: The start and end of an experience are the most critical points in determining a person's memory of the event.
dos: Ensure the first and last interactions are high quality and satisfying ('Start strong, End strong').
donts: Don't neglect the final steps of a user journey such as confirmation screens or logout.

characteristics: Most users hold phones one-handed with the thumb anchored at the bottom-right.
dos: Place primary actions at the bottom of the screen; design for a natural sweeping thumb arc.
donts: Place frequently used buttons in the 'Ow' zone (top-left/top-right); assume users will change their grip to reach controls.

characteristics: The degree of reorientation a person experiences when moving between pages or touchpoints.
dos: Design for coherency across all touchpoints; bridge gaps so users don't have to.
donts: Force users to re-learn navigation on every page; provide inconsistent experiences across different channels (web/phone).

characteristics: Users are smart but busy; they move from being 'noobs' to intermediates and need relevant info at each stage.
dos: Focus design efforts on intermediates; provide clear labels rather than abstract icons.
donts: Overwhelm first-time users with 'coach marks' or instructional overlays; treat users as experts from the first interaction.

characteristics: Contrast, size, and depth direct the user's attention to the most important elements.
dos: Make important buttons higher contrast; make primary actions larger.
donts: Make the logo the biggest element unless it is the primary goal; use high contrast for secondary/background tasks.

principle: Start with a feature not a layout
characteristics: Design actual functionality instead of the application shell (nav bars, sidebars, logos).
dos: Start with a piece of actual functionality like 'searching for a flight'.
donts: Don't design the navigation bar or top nav first.

characteristics: Avoid designing every single feature and edge case before implementation.
dos: Work in cycles; build a simple version of the next feature and make it real.
donts: Don't try to figure out every edge case (e.g., 2000 contacts) using only a design tool.

characteristics: Reduce decision fatigue by defining restrictive systems in advance.
dos: Choose from a pre-defined set of 8-10 shades or a specific type scale.
donts: Don't reach for the color picker or tweak font sizes one pixel at a time manually.

principle: Establish a spacing and sizing system
characteristics: Use a constrained set of values rather than arbitrary pixel counts.
dos: Use a non-linear scale (e.g., multiples of 16 px) where jumps are at least 25%.
donts: Don't nitpick between 120 px and 125 px.

characteristics: Limit the number of font sizes used in an interface.
dos: Use a hand-crafted scale (e.g., 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 24, 30...); use rem or px units.
donts: Don't use em units for nested font sizes; avoid mathematical modular scales that create fractions.

characteristics: Action buttons should be designed based on hierarchy (Primary, Secondary, Tertiary).
dos: Use solid, high-contrast colors for primary actions; use links for tertiary.
donts: Don't design buttons purely on semantics (e.g., making every 'Delete' button big and red).

characteristics: Use spacing to connect related elements and separate distinct groups.
dos: Ensure there is more space around a group than there is within it.
donts: Don't use the same margin below a label as you do below the input field.

characteristics: Primary consideration for all design decisions to help users carry out tasks quickly.
dos: Structure products so tasks are completed fast. Use skeuomorphic touches for older, less tech-savvy users.
donts: Don't ignore the specific demographics of the target audience.

characteristics: The physical space and platform (TV vs. Mobile) change usage variables.
dos: Consider viewing distance and input methods (e.g., remote for TV). Adjust text size and contrast for the specific environment.
donts: Don't design for TV the same way you design for a mobile phone.

characteristics: Layout, color, and typography influence where the eye is drawn.
dos: Use high contrast and bold type for important items. Place items in the order you want them consumed.
donts: Don't make all elements equal in weight; users won't know where to look first.

characteristics: Most information is conveyed through text, requiring legible sizes and leading.
dos: Use at least 12pt font (16pt is better). Keep line lengths between 45-75 characters.
donts: Don't accept default font settings; ensure leading is at least 1.5x type size.

principle: Color and Shape have specific meanings
characteristics: Norms like green/ticks for 'good' and red/crosses for 'bad' are established.
dos: Use yellow triangles for warnings and blue circles for information.
donts: Don't jumble these norms as it causes user confusion. Don't rely on color alone for accessibility.

characteristics: Designs should be concise to avoid overwhelming users.
dos: Show only relevant context; use progressive disclosure for more details.
donts: Don't display all content at once if it isn't necessary for the immediate task.

principle: People identify objects by recognizing patterns
characteristics: The brain uses 24 basic geometric shapes (geons) to identify objects.
dos: Use grouping and white space to create patterns; use simple geometric drawings for icons.
donts: Favor 3D elements over 2D, as 3D may slow recognition.

principle: People scan screens based on past experience and expectations
characteristics: Users look at the center first, avoiding edges, and follow their culture's reading patterns.
dos: Place important information in the top third or middle of the screen.
donts: Put important content at the edges of the screen.

principle: People believe that things that are close together belong together
characteristics: Proximity implies relationship, following Gestalt principles.
dos: Put items in close proximity to show they are related; use whitespace instead of lines to group.
donts: Ignore spacing between unrelated items, which can cause confusion.

principle: What you see isn't what your brain gets
characteristics: The brain interprets sensory input based on expectations and creates shortcuts like the Kanizsa triangle.
dos: Influence perception using shapes and colors; consider user background and familiarity.
donts: Assume users see a realistic representation of what is physically there.

characteristics: Depend on visual representation of objects, physical actions instead of syntax, and immediate visible effects.
dos: Provide immediate feedback; allow actions to be reversed; align interface actions with user understanding.
donts: Use complex syntax; force users to use logical conditions for simple tasks.

characteristics: Battery-powered devices connect intermittently to save power, leading to status discontinuities.
dos: Handle network outages gracefully; explain to users why devices might be out of sync.
donts: Assume constant connectivity; show conflicting status information without explanation.

characteristics: As the flexibility of a system increases, its usability often decreases.
dos: Strike a balance between feature richness and ease of use; use secondary devices for complex rare tasks.
donts: Bloat a simple device interface with capabilities that are rarely needed.

characteristics: Users experience doubt, regret, or anxiety after a purchase (internal inconsistency).
dos: Reassure customers after a sale; provide honest information pre-purchase.
donts: Share insufficient information that leads to misaligned expectations.

characteristics: Habits are automatic behaviors triggered by a cue and reinforced by a reward.
dos: Identify a clear, unambiguous cue; provide an immediate meaningful reward.
donts: Expect users to repeat complex, varying tasks to form a habit; use ambiguous triggers.

principle: People imagine objects tilted and at a slight angle above
characteristics: The 'canonical perspective' is the universal way people remember and recognize objects.
dos: Draw icons and objects from a perspective slightly above and offset to the side.
donts: Show objects from a top-down or direct 2D perspective if fast recognition is needed.

principle: People can miss changes in their visual fields
characteristics: Inattention blindness occurs when users are focused on a task and don't expect changes.
dos: Add visual cues like blinking or beeps to signal a refreshed screen change.
donts: Assume a user noticed a change just because it appeared on the screen.

principle: Red and blue together are hard on the eyes
characteristics: Chromostereopsis causes depth perception issues where one color jumps out and another recedes.
dos: Maintain high contrast between foreground and background.
donts: Place blue and red or green and red near each other; use blue text on a red background.

principle: Standardized paper sizes (DIN) facilitate storage and impact.
characteristics: Standard sizes (A-series) fit into files, indexes, and standard envelopes efficiently.
dos: Employ standardized DIN system sizes for printed matter.
donts: Use custom sizes that result in paper waste and increased production costs unless absolutely necessary.

principle: Marginal proportions influenced by the Golden Section enhance reading pleasure.
characteristics: A well-proportioned margin provides technical safety for trimming and aesthetic relief.
dos: Use mathematical formulas like the Golden Section to calculate margins; make margins wide enough to hold the book without covering text.
donts: Make margins too narrow (risk of text mutilation during trimming) or exactly equal (creates dullness).

principle: The fewer the differences in the size of the illustrations, the quieter the impression created by the design.
characteristics: Uniformity in visual elements reduces visual noise and promotes a sense of calm and order.
dos: Reduce pictorial elements to a few formats of the same size.
donts: Use many different picture sizes unless intended to create a restless, high-energy effect.

principle: Program to an Interface not an Implementation
characteristics: Depend on abstractions rather than concrete classes to ensure flexibility for future extensions.
dos: Determine what an object needs from another; describe methods in an interface/abstract class; make dependencies implement that interface.
donts: Make classes tightly coupled to concrete employee or collaborator classes.

characteristics: Users typically scan digital content from top to bottom along the left edge.
dos: Align buttons and Call-to-Action elements along the left edge or at the bottom of descriptive content.
donts: Place the strongest visual element in the middle of a scan path, forcing eyes to travel back and forth.

characteristics: Reducing the number of options helps users focus on the primary task.
dos: Limit formatting options in editors to encourage writing; use single-column layouts for focus.
donts: Overwhelm users with infinite settings or buttons that distract from core purpose.

characteristics: Elements should have a visual volume corresponding to their importance.
dos: Use high-contrast typography for major headers; use 'Scream' modules for promotions and 'Whisper' for tips.
donts: Make secondary information as loud as primary calls to action.

characteristics: Prioritizing long-term utility and accessibility over current stylistic trends.
dos: Stick to proven UI patterns; prioritize performance over flashy effects (e.g., parallax).
donts: Adopt new technologies or styles purely for novelty if they hinder the user experience.

characteristics: Users are motivated by the presence and feedback of others.
dos: Show discussion threads; display learner counts; use visible testimonials.
donts: Hide social interactions in areas intended for collaborative learning.

principle: People reconstruct memories every time they remember them
characteristics: Memories are not static; they change over time and the most vivid ones are often erroneous.
dos: Focus design on the memory that will remain rather than just the immediate experience; use 'start strong, end strong' strategies.
donts: Don't assume user feedback after a task is a 100% accurate representation of the literal events.

principle: Anecdotes are more persuasive than data
characteristics: Personal stories have more power to influence human belief and memory than raw data points.
dos: Use storytelling and user scenarios to communicate design value and persuade stakeholders.
donts: Don't present data in isolation without a narrative context.

characteristics: Surprising users with positive, unexpected value creates a much stronger mental impression than standard service.
dos: Incorporate 'Easter eggs' or helpful unexpected alerts (e.g., Gmail's missing attachment reminder).
donts: Not in source

principle: Animation is a tool for brand messaging
characteristics: Motion conveys personality, tone, and brand perspective, similar to color and typography.
dos: Establish a consistent motion vocabulary that reflects brand attributes; use custom easing curves.
donts: Use mismatched or arbitrary animations that create a 'mish-mash' experience; use default OS easing for branded elements.
